channel.title: Courtney A. Shearer
channel.description: Recent content on Courtney A. Shearer

item.description:
<!-- --- title: "My first structured jacket: lessons from linen" date: 2024-02-20 tags: ["sewing"] summary: "Attempting a tailored linen jacket taught me more about garment construction than any YouTube tutorial ever could." ---
Attempting a tailored linen jacket taught me more about garment construction than any YouTube tutorial ever could.
I've been sewing for about three years, mostly knit fabrics and loose-fitting wovens — the forgiving stuff. But this spring I decided to push myself: a structured jacket in a rust-colored heavy linen.
## Why linen is both perfect and punishing
Linen is beautiful. It presses like a dream, drapes with weight and intention, and the texture has this satisfying depth that cotton just doesn't have. But it also frays aggressively and has zero stretch, which means fitting has to be precise.
I used the [Closet Core Jasika Blazer](https://closetcorepatterns.com) pattern, which has excellent instructions but assumes you know what you're doing with tailoring. Reader, I did not fully know what I was doing.
## What I learned about interfacing
The biggest revelation was how much interfacing changes everything. I'd always used fusible interfacing sparingly — a collar here, a waistband there. But a structured jacket is *mostly* interfacing. The chest piece, the under-collar, the front facing...
Sew-in hair canvas behaves completely differently from fusible. It has a bias that you work with, not against. Pad-stitching the lapels by hand took two evenings and I now completely understand why bespoke tailoring costs what it costs.
## The bound buttonholes saga
I made four bound buttonholes. The first was terrible. The second was okay. The third was pretty good. The fourth I'm genuinely proud of.
There's probably a metaphor here about learning curves and scientific method but I'll let you draw your own conclusions.
## Would I do it again?
Absolutely yes. The jacket fits well, looks intentional, and taught me skills I'll use forever. Next up: wool crepe trousers.
*(Photos coming once I actually hem the sleeves.)* -->

<!-- --- title: "Why I fell in love with systems biology" date: 2024-01-15 tags: ["science"] summary: "Systems biology isn't just biology with math sprinkled on top — it's a fundamentally different way of asking questions about life." ---
Systems biology isn't just biology with math sprinkled on top — it's a fundamentally different way of asking questions about life.
When I started my PhD, I thought I knew what I was getting into: build models, run simulations, publish papers. But within the first few months I realized systems biology is really a *philosophy* about how to study complexity.
## The reductionist trap
Classical biology has been incredibly successful by being reductionist — isolate a gene, knock it out, see what breaks. This approach gave us the central dogma, the genetic code, CRISPR. It works.
But it has limits. A cell isn't just a collection of parts. It's an emergent system where the *relationships* between parts matter as much as the parts themselves. A protein doesn't just have a function; it has a function *in context*.
## Networks change everything
When you look at biological systems as networks — protein-protein interactions, gene regulatory networks, metabolic pathways — you start to see patterns that are invisible at the single-gene level. Hubs. Modularity. Robustness.
This is what drew me in: the idea that we can use network structure to make predictions, not just descriptions.
## What I'm working on now
My current work sits at the intersection of machine learning and experimental design. The question I keep coming back to: *how do we optimally learn about a biological system when experiments are expensive?*
Active learning, Bayesian optimization, and multi-modal data integration are all part of my toolkit. But the biological intuition has to drive the modeling choices — not the other way around.
More on this soon. -->

<!-- --- title: "Machine Learning Approaches for Optimal Experimental Design in Biological Systems" date: 2024-01-01 venue: "In progress · Harvard SSQB" authors: "**Courtney A. Shearer**, et al." abstract: "Developing active learning frameworks to minimize the number of experiments required to learn accurate models of biological systems, with applications to gene regulatory networks." --- -->

item.title: Curriculum Vitae
item.description:
Download PDF ↓

Education

2021 – 2025
Harvard University, Cambridge MA
PhD, Systems, Synthetic & Quantitative Biology (SSQB)
Thesis: Generative Evolutionary Models for Variant Effect Prediction
Advisor: Debora Marks
2015 – 2019
Clemson University, Clemson SC
BS, Computer Science · Minor Genetics · Magna Cum Laude · Honors College

Experience

Oct 2025 – present
Google DeepMind, London UK
Research Engineer — 9-month visit to the Genomics Team within the Science Unit
2021 – 2025
Debora Marks Lab, Harvard, Cambridge MA
PhD Candidate — Clinical variant effect prediction, therapeutic design, DNA/RNA language modeling, generative sequence models, protein language models
Aug 2020 – Aug 2021
CRISPR Therapeutics, Boston MA
Bioinformatics Engineer — GACT Computational Biology Team. Analyzed high-throughput CRISPR screens; tech lead for Novaseq/UMI deduplication pipeline; refactored CIRCLEseq off-target detection pipeline
Jun 2019 – Jul 2020
Zymergen, San Francisco CA
Bioinformatics Engineer — Genome assembly/annotation pipelines at scale; dockerized bioinformatics tools; co-developed WGS variant pipeline; inaugural Scrum Master for Genome Analysis team
Summer 2017, 2018
Ancestry, Lehi UT / San Francisco CA
Software Developer Intern — Full stack web development; built video upload service with Spring Boot/AWS S3; rewrote full stack app from C# to Java
2015 – 2019
Feltus & Smith Labs, Clemson, Clemson SC
Bioinformatics / ML Researcher — NGS workflow development; deep learning for cancer tumor classification from RNAseq; co-first author publication in Nature Scientific Reports
Summer 2016
Conant Lab, NSF REU, Missouri
Bioinformatics REU Student — Metabolic network analysis for sheep feed efficiency; resulted in publication in Journal of Animal Science
